title: How to build agents when the smartest AI isn't smart enough
transcript: We think actually agents will be able to like speed up the time from kind of initial discovery to bringing a drug to patients by 2x. Today I'm talking to Nick Stone who is head of AI at Benchling, the platform that life sciences teams have recorded experiments on for 14 years. Now they're building agents on top of it. What we've seen is when you kind of put these models on top of the right data, the quality answers go way up. He breaks down how Benchling's agents for scientists are unlike typical coding agents. We actually still rely very heavily on uh SQL, right? We do some tricks around, you know, embedding table names and descriptions so that we can have kind of like faster paths to get them to query the right thing. and he explains why and how when it comes to eval leans on production traces. >> The types of questions that people ask are so specific to their science. It's very hard for us to to build evals. We spent a lot of time actually looking at traces in production throughout. Nick shares some unique perspectives on agents that may challenge your assumptions. Some people say like LM can't do anything novel, right? Their next token predictors. I think people just like haven't experimented enough with this. Welcome to Max Agency, the podcast that goes deep into how the best agents are being built by builders like you. Benching has been a company for 14 years. You guys launched Benchling AI 6 months ago. What is Benchling AI and how does it fit into the overall platform that you guys have been building? >> Yeah, so Benchling is a platform for life science R&D organizations to kind of store and manage their data. So this is everything from the experiments that they're running to the actual kind of like physical things that they're building in the lab to helping automate their their instruments uh to helping do kind of data analysis on top of that. And so the the core of benchling is this platform to help manage data and benching AI is the the layer on top of that to bring intelligence uh to scientists who want to interact with their data. So it helps them find data, design experiments, analyze data, um kind of accelerate each part of the the scientific journey. >> What does it look like? How do I how do I use it? >> Yeah, so it's a a chat interface in Benchling. So scientists log in uh and then we have an agent kind of backing this chat interface that can interact with objects within Benchling. So for example uh let's imagine you're a scientist who is kind of working in in a normal drug discovery company and you've run some experiments. The main question on your mind is what should I do next? Uh you you log into benchling you can type in your question. It might be hey has anyone done a similar experiment to me in the past. It could be hey help me design this kind of next experiment. It could be hey help me analyze the data from my last experiment. uh and kind of our agent will go kind of do whatever the task that you've asked for uh and then return the the results uh in an interpretable way and kind of seamlessly integrated so you can kind of save it back into the platform and kind of continue on with with your job. How long horizon are these tasks supposed to be? Is it like question answer you get something back in 5 seconds or is it you know kick something off 15 minutes later it ends? >> Yeah, it's a range. Uh it depends on what you're looking for. Sometimes it's simple just data retrieval, right? Go find something and kind of give me that answer really quickly. Sometimes they're much harder tasks that take yeah tens of minutes. Um a good example of this is around report writing. A lot of what scientists do is kind of generate data in order to you know uh try and get to the the truth of the underlying biology. Uh but eventually you need to kind of like prove that to someone else. That could be um kind of a partner organization that you're working with. It could be internal to your manager. It could be uh a regulatory agency like the FDA. In order to do that, you need to kind of consolidate kind of large amounts of data that you've maybe gathered over years uh and produce it in a often standardized format. So, for example, there's something called an IND, an investigational new drug application that you submit to the FDA. Uh it's very standardized. It's usually thousands of pages. you know, compiling all of that historically takes months. AI on top of the right data, crucially, the right data can, you know, give you parts of that within, as you're saying, 15, 20 minutes. So, uh, it really depends on the task, but we we see a wide range of, um, difficulty in the task that people are asking about. You mentioned data. Like how big of an advantage is it that you guys have had this data platform for a decade plus and are now starting to build agents on top of it as opposed to every other agent startup which I feel like has come out in the past 2 years and is starting from the agent and then probably trying to get data in after the fact. I would say that's one of our core advantages actually. So I spend a lot of time talking to scientists and you know asking them about their opinions on AI trying to get them to use AI and a lot of scientists tried chatt when it came out they asked about their specific science right and they got pretty bad answers maybe it was hallucinating maybe it was just like not relevant or maybe it was kind of like ignorant answers. Uh what we've seen is when you kind of put these models on top of the right data, the quality answers go way up, right? And because it scientists are like focused often on a very narrow area, it's somewhere in the pre-training data. But if you can contextualize that and kind of put the the right data in front of the agent, it's going to do much better than a generic agent even with tools to query outside systems, right? So like MCP obviously uh you know is very powerful but there's some limitations uh there. We think kind of having that data up front can both speed up agents to get you the answer faster but then also just give you much higher quality answers. >> Interesting. This maybe gets to some of the agent architecture in the hood but like like how does it speed that up? What is the difference between just giving it via MCP to to some other agent? I mean part of it is just you know we're building our tools uh with a knowledge of our architecture uh such that you know the the agent can make best use of that but part of it is MCP is just kind of a limited view into other systems right so if you're taking like a very naive view you only get what those kind of third party systems allow you to get that's often not all of the the tools or all of the data that you would need in order to do well so kind of by building it from the inside out we have the advantage of being able to access any kind of relevant piece of data uh and also being able to change the underlying system such that uh we can make it better and we can build new tools on top of the system we actually change. So I think like MCP as a protocol makes a lot of sense and it's great and we are both an MCP server and an MCP client but there's some limitations uh versus what you can do kind of like inside the agent. Have you found that you've adapted the platform as you build your agent to change the platform in some way to make it better for the agent? Yeah, I mean maybe a simple example of this is you know pre-AII we have a standard search platform uh but obviously with the you know AI coming out we've done a bunch of things to kind of change our underlying search platform to make it better uh for agents we've kind of changed our chunking strategy we've changed uh some of our storage on on the back end obviously like embedding search is very important so there's kind of like underlying search platform improvements which also go to the humans right they they actually get better search a lot of times uh but we've kind of made those underlying platform changes because we know that agents will be able to take advantage of it. >> I imagine search is pretty important for a lot of these research style agents. How much has have you focused on improvements to the search versus improvements to the agent and has that changed over the past 6 months or I I feel like there's a big trend recently in like agentic search and like really changing the agents architecture for search as opposed to making the search go from like 97 to 98 or something like that. But I'm it sounds like you have made changes. So I'm curious how you've approached that. >> One kind of benefit of us being 14 years old is we have lots of engineering teams working on lots of things, not just agents, right? So our team very much focuses on the agents, but we work very closely with our our search team. So we have a dedicated search team, right, who's uh managing our search. And so that lets us both focus on improvements to the agents as well as working with other teams who are actually improving the the search directly. >> Okay, let's talk about the agents. What do they what does it look like under the hood? Honestly, pretty standard agent loop, right? LM calling tools. I think a lot of the the interesting aspects of it are how we build the tools, the skills that we put in, and some of the the context management, but at the end of the day, right, it's kind of just a LM in a loop uh with a bunch of tools and a kind of coding sandbox attached. >> Cool. Okay. You mentioned a few things. You mentioned skills. Is this skills in the same sense as like coding skills? Like do you use that same paradigm? Yeah. So, we uh conform to the agent skills spec and we both support users bringing their own skills as well as us kind of building what we call kind of like built-in or native skills to the to the agent. >> Do you use a coding agent harness under the hood? Then, >> no. Uh we've built our own harness in part because I think coding agent harnesses are not the right solution for science. Um we haven't done as much kind of experimentation here as I would like. We're starting to to do a bit more here, but I think there's a lot of problems in science and a lot of the questions that our users are asking are not good kind of uh like onetoone comparisons that to what coding agents are good at. For example, Google last year came out with their co-scientist paper where they had like a radically different harness where you know in order to come up with more novel hypotheses, they would have this like tournament style play of LLMs kind of uh proposing ideas and kind of having a greater look at two different uh proposals and then choose the best one and doing that iteratively which is very different from like a coding style harness. You could probably you know force a coding style harness into that. Uh but I I think that's kind of an example in my mind of like oh actually this is like a specific scientific task that looks very different from hey I have a spec and I want to like write a bunch of code against it. I'm of the belief that there is still value in having kind of your own harness uh and not being kind of wedded to the decisions that make uh something really good at writing code. I would say writing code is an important part of this, but a lot of the the kind of queries that we get involve like writing no code actually. >> And so you mentioned code sandboxes and so it sounds like your agent does write code. You're just saying it does things beyond writing code and and that is what requires the other. >> Yeah. And writing code is very helpful, right? It's very helpful to manipulate files. It's helpful to do data analysis which obviously is kind of an important task in science. uh but that is only some fraction of what people are asking for and so I see it more as you know coding is an important ability for this agent but it's not the the driving force of this agent. You mentioned like tournament style things. Do you guys ha do you guys do that? Do you have like multi- aent architectures in in play already? >> We do a few different things. So um one kind of example that we are quite interested in is how to take unstructured data and turn it into structured data. So a lot of scientific data unfortunately comes in the form of PDFs uh Excel spreadsheets um files from instruments that are not well structured and you know a big kind of advantage of benching as a platform is we help uh companies define their their data model and we give them this place to pl put structured data. Uh unfortunately the disconnect is it historically has taken a lot of work to go from you know maybe a 10 or 100page PDF and kind of like enter that data into a kind of structured table. So we obviously look to AI to help with this kind of bringing in the right data is really important like if you get that wrong everything downstream is kind of like irrelevant. And so uh the companies we work with are are very sensitive to you know having high quality data import. And so actually one of the the first things we ever built as a company on the AI front was uh this kind of data entry agent that uses multiple models under the hood uh where we give the same problem to multiple different model families and then we cross compare the results because what we saw was uh if two models uh kind of disagree there's usually an error. Maybe it's sometimes one of them was right and one of them made an error, but often times they both get it wrong or or there's something that a human should look at. And if they both agree, that usually is good enough data quality. And so, uh, we've kind of explored that specifically in this space. Um but we started exploring it for kind of non-data entry tasks as well. Maybe harder scientific questions where uh you know sending the same question to multiple different model families and cross-co comparing the answers can give you a higher quality result than just asking kind of the either one model or one model multiple times. >> You mentioned like model families. Are you basically saying that like Haiku and Sonnet and Opus they all tend to make the same types of mistakes? Yeah, I I would say um when I say model families, I mean different model providers, not just different size models within the providers. I think of this as kind of like spiky intelligence, right? Like Claude has different advantages to GPT has different advantages to Gemini, right? And so um each of them will make slightly different errors whether it's Opus versus Sonnet Haiku. Obviously there's different quality within that. Uh but actually being able to ask different model providers uh we found gives us kind of much better performance especially on these data entry tasks but uh we're starting to see it in other tests as well. >> Do you guys know like which types of things certain models are good at and route there accordingly or is it more like we're going to route to all three? We don't really have a great taxonomy on which ones are better but like we're we're going to do exactly what you said earlier. >> Yeah, right now we route to all of them. Um and we kind of cross compare. We think there's advantage there. There are certain tasks where we'll choose the right model family. So, Gemini tends to be very good with images uh is what we found. And so, you know, for specific tasks involving images, we might route to that. Um there's obviously like speed cost considerations where we'll route to specific models. But specifically for this aspect where I'm talking about like improving performance on a task, I think you actually need to to go kind of multimodel and spend more tokens. How many of your tasks are like data entry seems like one that's pretty verifiable in terms of like did it extract the right thing or did it not extract the right thing? Writing a research paper sounds maybe way less verifiable. What percentage of them like are really verifiable versus not really verifiable. Yeah, I'd say most tests in science are less verifiable. Um and even the data entry one, you know, if you get sufficient data, it's pretty hard for a human to verify. I' I'd say it's actually easier for them to verify than it is for them to enter and then verify. So, we definitely think there's an advantage there, but um it can be somewhat challenging to to verify. Even uh the report writing that I was talking about earlier, you will have a human verify that right before you submit it to the FDA. It's just like so high stakes that that will happen. And so, what what we've seen is AI is very good at producing that first draft. You're still going to have a human uh verify it. You can actually have another AI verify it. Uh so we've seen this pattern a lot where like maybe kind of you use uh benchling AI to write the report and then you open a kind of fresh session, put in that report and have another agent kind of double check it but there's still a lot of kind of manual verification and then there's the kind of set of tasks which are basically unverifiable because it's you're on that bleeding edge of science and like the way to verify it is you go run an experiment that takes weeks or months and hundreds of thousands of dollars and people are doing that right. We see people asking questions of the agent, you know, maybe it's suggesting some experiments that they should run. They have some intuition, right? Uh about like what might work, what might not work, but there's no ground truth, right? The ground truth means you actually have to go run that in the lab, and that's actually quite expensive to verify. >> How do you guys do eval? >> We do do eval. Uh I'd say we spend a lot of time kind of thinking about eval verify, right? So, can you find the right data, right? So if you're finding the wrong data kind of everything downstream of that's not going to be good entering data, right? There's some verification there. Uh but then we spend a lot of time actually looking at traces in production because the the types of questions that people ask are so specific to their science. It's very hard for us to to build evals. We are working actually on some stuff that we're we're hoping to release soon about kind of building better evals to uh that are more realistic for the types of problems that scientists see in industry as opposed to maybe some more academic benchmarks. At the end of the day, we talk to users and we look at kind of production traffic uh because it's pretty hard to build evals for kind of novel scientific discovery. I feel like looking at production traffic is so key to like tuning the agent once you release it. What is your guys' process for that? Who looks at the traces? How do you decide which traces to look at? Like what is that process? So, everyone on the team looks at the traces. I I would say um we have a few different patterns for this. So, one is we have like a a weekly fire chief rotation who's kind of uh addressing issues. As part of that, they are asked to look at traces uh and bring them to our kind of like weekly uh tech operations meeting and share that with the team. So there's kind of like a kind of like recurring uh hey everyone look at traces. Uh we also get feedback from users. So they say thumbs up, thumbs down. Uh and that's kind of like a good external signal to to look at traces. And then I would say people who are working on specific features, right? Are going to go look at the traces and you know our product managers, our engineers uh who are who are building something uh will actually go and see how people are using that feature after releasing it or kind of putting it into beta or something like that. Early on when you were talking about the agent, you mentioned kind of like tool design and context engineering. What have you guys learned over the past few months year in in in that vein? I would say this is in some ways like the key part of making your agent good. It's challenging. Longer context windows help uh but they're certainly not a solution. We also have seen the the industry move more towards the the harnesses that are very popular, right? these coding agent harnesses, you know, having virtual file systems because it mimics like how a coding agent works on your local file system. Uh, so we've started to adopt some of those techniques as well. We also, as you mentioned, are 14 years old, right? And we we've kind of built, you know, a whole infrastructure around our SQL database and taking advantage of that. And so I think sometimes it makes sense to to follow the crowd and do maybe more file-based things, but we actually still rely very heavily on uh SQL, right? because you know the the models are quite good at writing SQL. Uh and so we do some tricks around uh you know embedding table names and descriptions so that we can have kind of like faster paths to get them to query the right thing. But I would say it's kind of a mix of trying to keep up with the latest tools where we think the models will will be good at uh while still kind of taking advantage of some of the the architecture that that we've built over the last 14 years. You mentioned before that you're not using a coding agent harness and you actually don't think that's necessarily the right kind of like uh solution for the space. I feel like the labs have been talking a lot about how they're rlinging models for their harnesses which are coding agent harnesses. Have you observed any negative or adverse kind of like effects because they're being overly tuned for those harnesses yet? >> I don't think I've seen it yet. It's possible it's too early, right? we we might see that in the next generation u of models but I don't think I've actually observed any of that uh and I think you know when we eval kind of our overall system we look at different models right and so if you start to see future generations of models getting worse with our harness I think there there's like a important discussion for us to have internally of like do we think we move to a different model that's going to do better with our harness or do we change the harness to to better fit the model. And I actually don't know what what we would end up doing here. >> You mentioned that you guys use a bunch of different models before. When someone kicks off a run in Benchling AI, how many different models might it hit under under that run? Depends what it's doing. Uh I'd say like upper limit is probably seven or eight. Uh I'd say most of the runs are maybe only one to two. We typically have like one main driver model for for a kind of core harness. Uh but it'll certainly call out to usually at least one other model and then depending on the complexity of the task, maybe several other different models. I feel like a a big part of bio is running experiments and learning from that. Do you also think of these agents as learning from what they're seeing their users do? Or are you thinking of memory for your agents in any way? >> We're I'd say still early here. Um, one of the things we we've recently released was this ability to have agent skills uh within the platform and we think this is actually really powerful uh primitive for scientists. Of course, we we've added the ability for our agents to be able to create and update those skills. So, I would say that's like maybe the the first maybe simplest layer of memory is just, hey, you did a task. the next time I need to do this task, I want you to be able to do it in a similar way and hopefully do it much faster and with higher fidelity and better >> and and are those skills like will the user say that exactly what you said like hey remember this or is the agent kind of like you know seeing that the user did it three times in the background and doing thinking to itself that it should create its own skill. >> Yeah. So uh the agent actually has the ability to create its own skill. It's been tuned and we've prompted it such that it it's pretty biased towards a user instantiating though it could do that. We are also um in the process of adding the ability for agents to run in the background there. I think you could start to do a little bit more of what you're talking about of hey I've looked through your last 10 chats. I think you know I should create these three skills. Uh we've been a little conservative there because we don't want to create a bunch of skills that are not helpful. So it has the capabilities but it is kind of prompted to to be pretty responsive to what a user wants. >> And are these skills right now at a user level at an org level at some the user can choose whether to share it? How do you guys think about that? We have a concept of personal skills so users can create them for themselves. Uh and then you know since we have been around 14 years we've built a lot of permissioning into our platform. So we have the ability for users to then share those skills uh with other users or teams organizationally. Uh and then we also actually have as Benchling have been building skills and sharing them uh with kind of all of our users so that uh one we think they're they're kind of general purpose and and useful and we've done a bunch of work to you know learn from how people are using our agents and two they provide kind of an example for people who are maybe less familiar with skills of like what should a skill look like what could go into a skill. uh so yeah we we think the the organizational piece doesn't seem to have been solved broadly as like a ecosystem I would say as part of having a platform where people are already sharing things and working with teammates uh we think it's important to to build that directly in >> you mentioned background agents and potentially using those for memory are you also thinking of using them for other things >> we think of two types of background agents one is um kind of like schedule based you know every week give me a report because I have a discovery meeting. Uh, I think that's super useful. Scientists spend again a lot of time, you know, documenting things, building powerpoints, building kind of like documents to to share with others. If you can get a first draft out every week, that that's going to be very powerful. The second one, uh, is event based. So based on something that happens within the platform, can you trigger an agent to run? And I think that again is one of the advantages we have for being around for a lot of uh, years. People can do a lot of things in benching. And so we have a very rich um kind of interface where lots of different things are happening. So uh you could imagine for instance if you're a scientist, you're running something in the lab, you have an instrument in your lab that actually takes a measurement. Uh we've built over several years uh the ability to like take data from that machine, bring it to the cloud, right, and kind of automatically register it within bench, register those results. You can imagine that automatically triggering an agent to do analysis. So by the time you get back from your lab to your laptop, you don't just have the kind of clean data, you actually have the analysis of like, hey, here's what happened in your experiment and here's what I think you should do next. I think I'm kind of maybe most excited about some of those use cases where um you know, you as a scientist are already doing kind of your normal work and the agents really are in the background seamless, but just like getting you to the next stage that much faster. You've mentioned a lot of really cool and advanced features that are like reminiscent of a lot of things in coding agents. Your users are scientists. Like how are you adapting all like are they are they a do they understand all these things? How are you thinking about the UX? Like do they understand what skills are? Do they know how to think about skills? Do they know how to think about events background agents? How are you thinking about the UX for these for these agents? >> Yeah, we spend a lot of time on user education. I would say I think um you know sometimes being in Silicon Valley right you get caught up in uh you know everything that's going on and you don't realize that like not everyone has maybe the time uh or the inclination to experiment with all the the latest and greatest that that's coming out and so we spend a lot of time working with our users showing them what they can do listening to their problems making sure we're we're building the right things there um so take skills for example when I go and talk to scientists Um, I'd say it's maybe like 50/50 of whether or not they've heard of skills at all. Right? So there there's some people who like, yeah, I've heard of skills, uh, or maybe kind of my, you know, company has some enterprise coding agent or chat client and they are some concept of skills there, but rarely have they spent a lot of time building skills. And then there's, you know, half of people who just like have never kind of touched skills at all. And so, um, it's a matter of us saying like, hey, this is this thing, right? this uh way of working. Luckily, I think there's actually like a good parallel in science, which is an SOP, a standard operating procedure. I'll sometimes describe skills as like SOPs for agents, right? You you aren't guaranteed that a human following an SOP will do the right thing, but you know, you write the SOPs with the goal of them kind of doing exactly those steps. And you know, similar idea, I think, for skills. So, we spend a lot of our time doing that translation, I would say. So, do you guys have a forward deployed engineering team? Is that basically what this is? We might not call it that, but uh Benchling actually from the the beginning has had a large service organization because science is really complex and it's really varied. So no two companies are really doing exactly the same science. And so even when we deploy kind of core benchling, we spend a lot of time with the the customer customizing it to their needs. And so we're I'd say like pretty familiar with doing that. Uh, and I think in the AI era, we've quickly seen that it's not just give people AI and they'll know what to do and they'll immediately be more productive. Sometimes that happens, but often times it's a matter of, you know, deeply understanding their workflows, their bottlenecks, and kind of showing how that can map into the the tools that we're building. It seems like a lot of the architecture is similar in a lot of ways to to coding agents and how that's kind of like brought into this new domain. Have there been cases where you've seen something in a coding agent or coding harness and been like that is wrong that won't work for this domain like other things might like skills sure great let's take those but but this thing that's that that doesn't transfer some of the like rules or spec based development kind of testing driven doesn't work cuz you don't have a way to like verify in silicone right so like sometimes it feels like coding agents are really good when you can give them like a defined end condition uh maybe like this goal setting uh idea, the Ralph loop that has been more popular recently, your goal is like make a drug that is safe and efficacious in humans. Unfortunately, you can't verify that in silicon, right? And so, like that doesn't map super well to to what we're we're doing here. I think there's certain tasks that maybe are more kind of as you're saying verifiable in silicon, but in general, I'd say like those types of of patterns just don't map well because the speed of your loop is again like you physically have to go do the experiment in real life that's going to take days, weeks, months. I saw a tweet the other day, you might have retweeted it or something about how coding is the first domain that a lot of the model labs are kind of like building in and it seems like bio might be the second. If you know that tweet, like why do why do you think that might be the case? Two answers. One is those of us who work in the life sciences are are kind of blessed with uh working on problems that are really important like human health is kind of like universally important to everyone. Food security, right? like there there's kind of like many positive uh externalities. Uh and I think the model labs, you know, if they're building this kind of general intelligence, this super intelligence, you're going to have to like apply it to something that is really worthwhile. And I think like at the end of the day, to some extent, it all comes back to to life sciences and kind of like uh you know, improving human health. So I think there's that aspect of it. I think there there's also an aspect of a lot of researchers come out of the life sciences. So like Daario did his uh posttock in neuroscience. So like there is I think some kind of just like inherent interest and familiarity with that domain. So I I think it's a combination of both of those things. One of the things that you said before this to me that I thought was interesting was that working in life sciences is kind of similar to like working with agents in some way like the mental aspect of it. Could you could you expand on that? Yeah, I I think uh LLMs are are are funny, right? So, you know, in the history of software, we've been building kind of deterministic software, right? And I think there's a a certain mindset that people who are good at writing code have. And we we call ourselves software engineers, right? And so, like there's this like engineering mindset uh that is very productive when you're writing code. People have been trying to engineer biology for a long time. That's actually how I like first got interested in it. Biology just doesn't work that way. like we we are not good enough at understanding biology to uh make it do exactly what we want and we spend a lot of time you know building assays to kind of like understand what's happening in the underlying biology and we probe it and we perturb it and when I I see some of the the research that's coming out of model labs around interpretability it feels like they're actually doing biology research in some ways right they're they're kind of like poking the system that they don't fully understand and watching how it responds. And this is how biology works, right? Like scientists have been doing this in labs for uh you know o over a hundred years. And for me, it's kind of funny to think about actually sometimes the people who are maybe best at you know understanding LMS or using LMS are much more similar to scientists and potentially biologists than they would be to to software engineers. It's it's like this black box that you're kind of like probing and see what happens and then and then using that as experiments. >> Exactly. And uh many advantages in working in LLM, right? Like in theory we understand it all the way down even though kind of in practice there's still some uh lack of understanding. We understand biology far less, right? Like it's much harder system. It takes much longer to do experiments on. But if you look at, you know, some of these large training runs, right? They take weeks or months, right? And then you only see what happens at the end, right? It it's it feels more like biology than traditional software engineering ever has. What's the profile of builder that you've found most successful in building agents on your teams at at Benchling? >> Yeah, I think it's really product minded engineers at the end of the day, right? Like we're trying to solve problems for our our users who are scientists. Uh and it just requires someone who's, you know, one willing to talk to and listen to scientists about their problems and kind of deeply understand that. Uh, and then two, someone who's willing to kind of like work with LM, really deeply understand where they're strong, where they're weak, and then kind of merge those two things together. So, the people who are best at that don't necessarily have a science background. I actually don't think you need a science background to be effective here. Uh, but I would say it's more of this like product engineer archetype. When will agents discover a novel cure for a disease? >> That's a loaded question. In the the biotech industry, there's lots of discuss about uh discussions about like what is an AI discovered drug.
